segments.text: Now, we come to Sutta No. 4.8.79.
Now, the venerable Sariputta came to visit the exalted one.
As he sat at one side, the venerable Sariputta said this to the exalted one,
Pray Lord, what is the reason, what is the cause, why such and such a trait,
practiced by some person, turns out a failure.
Again Lord, what is the reason, what is the cause, why such and such a trait,
practiced by some person, does not turn out as he intended.
Pray Lord, what is the reason, why such and such a trait turns out as he intended.
Again Lord, what is the reason, what is the cause, why such and such a trait,
practiced by some person, prosperous beyond his intention.
And then the Buddha said, in this matter Sariputta,
Suppose someone comes to a recluse of Brahmin and makes him an offer saying,
Sir, say what you want by way of support, but he does not give him what he offered.
If this man diseases from that life and returns to this sort of life,
whatever trait he may practice, it turns out a failure.
Again in this matter Sariputta, suppose someone comes to a recluse of Brahmin
and makes him an offer saying, Sir, say what you want by way of support,
but he does not give him what he intended to give.
Then if this man diseases from that life and returns to this sort of life,
whatever trait he may practice, it does not turn out as he intended.
Then again Sariputta, suppose someone comes to a recluse of Brahmin
and makes him a similar offer and gives him what he intended to give.
Then if this man diseases and comes back to the human world,
whatever trait he may practice turns out as he intended.
Once more Sariputta, suppose someone comes to a recluse of Brahmin
and makes him a similar offer and gives him more than he intended to give.
Then if this man diseases from that life and returns to this sort of life,
whatever trait he may practice, it prosperous beyond his intention.
This Sariputta is the reason. This is the cause.
Why such and such a trait practiced by some person turns out a failure
or does not turn out as he intended or does turn out as he intended
or prosperous beyond his intention. That's the end of the Sutta.
Here the Buddha is talking about this person who does a trait,
that means he opens some business and when he opens, he does some business,
he has some expectation of a certain profit that he hopes to make
or he thinks that he should make a certain profit.
But the result can come out four ways.
One is it turns out a miserable failure, maybe becomes bankrupt.
Second one, maybe he expects to make, for example, $100 a month,
but he does not make as much as he intended, maybe he gets only $50 a month.
He expected to get $100 but he got only $50 as the second case.
The third case is he expects to make $100 a month
and he actually makes $100 a month as the third case.
The fourth case is even though he expects to make $100 a month,
he might make $200 a month.
So this is quite a valid thing in life.
You see sometimes two shops on the same road, on the same street selling the same goods.
One shop might do very well, another shop might not do well
or sometimes somebody opens a shop and then he goes bankrupt
and then he sells off the shop, another person comes back
and the second person comes, takes over the same shop
and maybe does the same business and then he prospers.
See each person's karma is different.
So the reason the Buddha said is because of somebody promising to give something
and whether he gives or not, there are four possibilities.
The first one, he says he intends to give something to a virtuous recluse
or Brahmin, that means renunciate among.
He promises to give something and then in the end he does not give anything at all.
So the result of that is when karma ripens, in the same way he does a business,
he intends to make something, he doesn't make anything, he goes bankrupt.
So the second case is a person promises to give something to a virtuous monk
and then he gives less than what he promised to give.
So the result is that when he does a business, he also gets less than what he intended to get.
The third one, he promised something and he gave exactly what he promised.
So when he does the business, the returns is also exactly what he expected.
The fourth one, he said he wanted to give something
but he gave much more to the virtuous monk, he gave much more than he intended to give.
So in the same way when he does in a later life, when he does a business,
then he gets much more profit than he thought he was going to get.
So this Sutta shows that we should not be careless with our words and our promises.
Perhaps if you make a promise to a person who is not virtuous,
who maybe has a lot of bad karma, then the result won't be so bad for you.
But if you are in dealings with a virtuous person, you have to be very careful
because if you do wrong to a virtuous person, then your karma is much worse than if to an ordinary person.
The next Sutta is 4.9.85, the Buddha said,
monks, these four persons are found existing in the world.
What for? He who is in darkness and bound for darkness,
he who is in darkness but bound for light,
he who is in light but bound for darkness,
and he who is in light and bound for light.
And how monks is a person in darkness bound for darkness?
In this case, a certain person is born in a low family,
the family of a scavenger or a hunter or a basket weaver
or wheelwright or sweeper or in the family of some wretched man
hard put to it to find a meal or earn a living where food and clothes are hard to get.
Moreover, he is ill-favoured, ugly, dwarfish, sickly, half-blind, crooked, lame or paralyzed
with hardly any food, without clothes, vehicle, without perfumes or flower garlands,
bed, dwelling or lights.
He lives in the practice of doing evil with body, speech and mind,
and so doing when body breaks up after death,
he is reborn in the waste, the woeful plains, the downfall in hell.
Thus, monks is the person who is in darkness and bound for darkness.
And how monks is a person in darkness but bound for light?
In this case, a certain person is born in a low family,
the family of a scavenger or a hunter or a basket weaver, etc., etc., as before.
He lives in the practice of doing good with body, speech and mind,
and so doing when body breaks up after death,
he is reborn in the happy lot in the heaven world.
And how monks is a person in light but bound for darkness?
In this case, a certain person is born in a high family,
a family of wealthy nobles or a family of wealthy Brahmins or of wealthy householders,
in a family that is rich, exceedingly rich of great possessions
with the support of a bounding wealth, in a family that abounds in wealth of crops,
and that man is well built, comely and charming, possessed of supreme beauty of form.
He is one able to get clothes, vehicle, perfumes and flower garlands, bed, dwelling and lights.
But he lives in the practice of doing evil with body, speech and thought.
So doing when body breaks up after death, he is reborn in the waste,
the woeful plains, the downfall in hell.
Thus, monks is the person who is in light but bound for darkness.
And how monks is a person who is in light and bound for light?
In this case, a person is born in a high family,
a family of wealthy nobles or a family of wealthy Brahmins or of wealthy householders,
in a family that is rich, exceedingly rich of great possessions, etc. etc.
He lives in the practice of doing good with body, speech and mind.
So doing when body breaks up after death, he is reborn in the happy lot in the heaven world.
Thus, monks is one who is in light and bound for light.
These monks are the four persons found existing in the world.
So in this Uttara, you see there are different kinds of people in this world.
There are some who, because of bad karma, they are born in a low family
where life is difficult and they are physically also they look ugly
and they hardly get food, etc.
And in spite of that now, because they don't know the dhamma, nobody taught them the dhamma,
they do evil.
They are having bad life and still they do evil.
And because of that, they will go down to the woeful plains, the three woeful plains,
the animal realm or ghost or hell.
But the second type of person, he is born to a poor family, despised, etc.
But because of good conditions, he does good karmas of body, speech and mind.
So because of doing good, perhaps he comes across the dhamma
and the dhamma teaches him to live a righteous life, a good life.
So he does good karma and after that he is born in heaven,
or at least in the human realm or something which is in a better condition than before.
Then the third one is a person in light but bound for darkness.
That means a person was born to a rich family and then he is also endowed with good physical features.
He is healthy, strong and good looking, etc.
But he does evil and does evil deeds.
And this is quite common people who have come from a rich family.
They have power, they have money and they misuse it and try to enjoy life too much,
get the mosa of life and so later they will have to suffer and go to the woeful plains.
But the fourth one is extremely good.
He has got good karma, born in a good family and he takes the trouble to study the dhamma,
listen to the sutras and so he guides himself on the right course.
He uses his wealth to do meritorious deeds.
So he is planting the good karma so that in the next life he will be reborn into a very good life again.
So we have to remember the sutra which I read earlier also where the Buddha said,
a few human beings will come back to the human plane or be born into the deva realm.
Most human beings, when we pass away, most of us, maybe 70, 80% of human beings
will be born into the woeful plains.
But that is that figure, this is speculation, will be born into the woeful plains
and not many will be reborn into the human realm and the deva realm.
So because of that now we have to be very careful.
Don't live an ordinary life like most people.
Don't be an ordinary person because the ordinary person doesn't get a good rebirth.
We have to make the best out of life and then we will get a good rebirth.
Make the best out of life not meaning an essential enjoyment but in the dhamma sense.
Now we come across a sutra which is a bit technical and has got to do about meditation.
monks, these four persons are found existing in the world.
But for the unshaken recluse, the blue lotus recluse, the white lotus recluse
and the recluse who is exquisite among recluses.
And how monks is a person an unshaken recluse?
Here in monks, a monk is a pupil, one who has entered the way.
He dwells aspiring for the unsurpassed rest from the toil.
Just like a raja's eldest son, a noble duly anointed,
one who is not yet anointed and has not come to security.
Even so, monks is the pupil who has entered the way,
one who dwells aspiring for the unsurpassed rest from the toil.
I'll stop a while here to explain this one.
This unshaken recluse refers to an Arya,
one who has attained one of the eight stages of Aryahood.
So he is unshaken, you'll never fall into the woeful plains.
And how monks is a person the blue lotus recluse?
Herein a monk, by destroying the asavas, has reached the release by mind,
the release by wisdom that is free from the asavas,
and having realized it, abides therein.
Yet does he not abide, experiencing with his own person
the eight deliverances?
That's the second person.
I'll stop here just to explain.
The second person is an arahan.
He has destroyed the asavas.
But he is an arahan who does not experience the eight deliverances.
Later, I will explain the eight deliverances
and to continue the sutta, the Buddha said.
Thus, monks is a person, a blue lotus recluse.
And how monks is a person, a white lotus recluse?
Herein a monk, by destroying the asavas, has reached the release by mind,
the release by wisdom that is free from the asavas,
and having realized it, abides therein.
And abides experiencing with his own person the eight deliverances.
I'll stop here a while.
The white lotus is also an arahan, just like the blue lotus recluse.
The white lotus recluse is an arahan,
but he experiences the eight deliverances.
The blue lotus, the second one, is an arahan,
but he does not experience the eight deliverances.
The white lotus recluse experiences the eight deliverances.
That's the fourth one.
And how monks is a person, a recluse, exquisite among recluses?
Herein a monk, when invited, enjoys a plentiful supply of robes.
But he gets little if uninvited.
So also as regards arms, food and lodging, if invited,
he enjoys a plentiful supply of requisites and medicines for use in sickness,
but he gets little if uninvited.
With whatsoever fellows in the holy life he dwells,
their behavior towards him in action of body, speech and mind is generally pleasing,
rarely displeasing.
Moreover, their presence to him are generally pleasing,
rarely displeasing.
Again, as to those afflictions which originate from vile,
phlegm, wind, from the union of bodily humors,
from changes of seasons, from stress of circumstances,
from personal assaults, or from the ripeness of one's karma,
such do not trouble him much.
He is free from sickness.
As to the four jhanas which belong to the higher mind
and lead to happy living in this very life,
he is a winner of them at will, without difficulty and without trouble.
By destroying the asabhas, he reaches the release by mind,
the release by wisdom, himself comprehending it, this very life,
and having attained it, he abides therein.
Thus, monks is a person a recluse exquisite among recluses.
Now, monks, if rightly speaking,
one would speak of the recluse exquisite among recluses,
it is just of me that he would rightly use the words,
for I, monks, when invited, enjoy a plentiful supply of robes, etc., etc.
So, these monks are the four persons found existing in the world.
So, stop here for a while to say,
there is the end of this sutra.
But this sutra has been used by some monks to say that
there is a type of arahan who attains liberation without the jhanas.
And the reason they called this is because of the blue lotus recluse,
because here it is said that the blue lotus recluse is an arahan
who does not experience the eight deliverances.
Now, what are the eight deliverances?
The eight deliverances are explained in Agutra Nikaya 8.7.66,
and the eight deliverances are called the eight vimoka.
The first one, the first deliverance,
that person is conscious of body and he sees forms,
he is conscious of his body and he sees forms.
The second one, personally unconscious of body,
that means he is not conscious of his own body,
and he sees forms exterior to himself.
The second one, he sees forms exterior to himself.
The third deliverance is thinking it is beautiful.
He is intent on that abiding.
The fourth one abides in the sphere of infinite space.
The fifth abides in the sphere of infinite consciousness.
The sixth in the sphere of nothingness.
The seventh in the sphere of neither perception or non-perception.
And the eighth is the cessation of perception and feeling.
So you see, the number four, five, six, seven and eight
refers to the arupa jhanas, and finally the cessation.
Now there are some monks who use this uttara
and say that the first three deliverance is equivalent to the four jhanas.
It is equivalent to the four jhanas because they think that
without the four jhanas you cannot obtain this being conscious of body,
he sees form externally and sees form internally
and sees form externally and abides in the beautiful etc.
But this is incorrect.
When you compare it to another sutra, which is a few sutras down the book,
the next sutra I am going to read is 4.9.89.
The Buddha said,
monks, these four persons are found existing in the world.
What for?
The unshaken recluse, the blue lotus recluse, the white lotus recluse
and the recluse who is exquisite among recluses.
Now the first one, the Buddha said,
how monks is a person at unshaken recluse?
Herein a monk is of right view, right thoughts, right speech, right action, right livelihood,
right effort, right mindfulness and right concentration.
Thus a person is an unshaken recluse.
And how monks is a person at blue lotus recluse?
Herein a monk is one of right view, right thoughts, right speech, right action,
right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness and right concentration
and also right knowledge and right release or right liberation.
Yet does he not abide experiencing with his own person the eight deliverances?
Thus a person is a blue lotus recluse.
And how monks is a person a white lotus recluse?
Similarly, he has right view of the eight factors of the iron eight-fold path
plus right knowledge and right release.
And he abides experiencing with his own person the eight deliverances.
This is the white lotus recluse.
And then the recluse exquisite among recluses is as before.
That means this monk, he gets a plentiful supply of robes, etc., etc.
And it's also an arahan.
Now, when you compare this Uttar with the previous one,
you find that the first one, the unshaken recluse,
he has the eight factors of the iron-fold path
but he does not have the other two, right knowledge and right release.
That means unshaken recluse is an arahan, but not an arahan.
This is like earlier one.
But the blue lotus recluse here, you can see, he has all the ten factors, you know,
the eight factors of the iron eight-fold path
plus right knowledge and right release.
That means he's an arahan.
But he does not experience the eight deliverances also.
He does not experience the eight deliverances.
That means he's the same as the earlier blue lotus recluse.
But here, it is spelled out that he has all the eight factors of the iron eight-fold path
which means that he has got right concentration
and right concentration is always defined in the sutras as the four jhanas
or one-pointedness of mind.
So, it is clear from this sutra, 89,
that the eight deliverances does not include the four jhanas.
This is the mistake made by some monks.
They did not see this sutra.
They only saw the earlier one, sutra 87,
and they say that the blue lotus recluse there
is an arahan who does not experience the eight deliverances.
Therefore, he does not have the four jhanas.
But in this sutra, 89,
it is clear that the blue lotus recluse has all the eight factors of the iron eight-fold path
plus right knowledge and right release.
And still, he does not experience the eight deliverances.
Which means the eight deliverances has nothing to do with the four jhanas.
Although it has got to do with the arupa jhanas,
it does not have anything to do with the rupa jhanas.
So, it is very important when we study the sutras
to study many sutras and compare them,
then only you can get a right view.
If you just take one sutra or two sutras,
it is very difficult to understand.
For example, sati patana.
Just take sati patana, the maha sati patana sutra.
Study that sutra.
I think you know all about sati patana
because there is a lot of sutras on the sati patana in the Sanghuta Nikhaya.
There is one chapter devoted only to sati patana.
It is called the sati patana Sanghuta.
And there, if you want to understand about sati patana,
you have to read that Sanghuta.
So many sutras there.
One explains sati patana from different angles.
Then you have a right view.
So, in the same way here, it is very clear
that this blue lotus in the second sutra,
he does not have the eight deliverances,
does not experience the eight deliverances,
but he has right concentration.
One of the factors of the Aryan A4 path,
which means that he has got the jhanas, the rupa jhanas.
So, there is nowhere in the sutras
which talks about dry vision arahan.
Never mentioned in the sutra about a dry vision arahan.
It is something that is only found in the commentaries,
the later books.
In our sutras, the Buddha said very clearly,
in one of the sutras I had read earlier,
that sati patana and asaka dagami,
the first two fruition persons,
they have perfect sila.
Anagami has got perfect sila and samadhi.
That means anagami must have the jhanas.
And the arahan has got perfect sila, samadhi and panya,
which means all arahans have perfect sila, samadhi and panya.
That means all arahans have perfect samadhi,
which is the jhanas.
And there is nowhere in the sutras,
you can find anywhere there, the Buddha said,
you can attain arahan wood without the jhanas,
one pointedness of mine.
Now we have come to sutra number 4.10.93.
The Buddha said,
monks, these four persons are found existing in the world.
What for?
Here in monks, a certain person is one
who gains tranquilization of the mind internally,
but does not gain the higher wisdom
from contemplation of states.
Here in again monks,
a certain person is one who gains the higher wisdom
from contemplation of states,
but does not gain tranquilization of the mind internally.
Here in again monks,
a certain person is one who gains neither of these things.
Yet again, a certain person is one who gains both.
These four persons are found existing in the world.
Then monks, he who gains tranquilization of the mind,
but not the higher wisdom from contemplation,
should make an effort to establish the one
and attain the other.
Then at some future time,
he is one who has gained both of these things.
Then monks, he who has gained the higher wisdom
from contemplation, but not tranquilization of the mind,
should make an effort to establish the one
and attain the other.
Then at some future time,
he is one who has gained both.
Then monks, he who has gained neither tranquilization of the mind,
nor the higher wisdom from contemplation,
should put forth intense desire, effort,
exertion, impulse, unobstruction,
mindfulness and attention for the attainment
of those profitable states.
Just as monks, when one's turban or head is a blaze,
for the extinguishing thereof,
one must put forth intense desire,
effort, exertion, impulse,
unobstruction, mindfulness and attention.
Even so, must that person put forth intense desire
and etc., etc.,
for the attainment of those profitable states.
Then at some future time,
he is one who has gained both tranquilization
of the mind internally
and the higher wisdom from contemplation of states.
So monks, he who has gained both these things
should make an effort to establish
just those profitable states
and further to destroy the asabhas.
These monks are the four persons found existing in the world.
This sutta is concerned with meditation
and here you find there are two things
that the Buddha says should be developed.
One is tranquilization of the mind,
which is in Pali is called Samatha
and the other one is contemplation or vipassana.
Sometimes people translate vipassana as
insight or wisdom,
but that is not correct because from here you can see
this is one of the sutras where it says
the higher wisdom from contemplation.
That means contemplation will lead to the wisdom.
So vipassana will lead to wisdom or insight.
So vipassana is not insight or wisdom,
but vipassana is contemplation.
And these are the two things that we have to do
when we meditate.
One is tranquilization of the mind,
the other one is contemplation
to give us the insight or the wisdom.
So there are four types of persons here.
The first one is a person who has gained
tranquilization, Samatha,
but he has not gained the wisdom from contemplation.
Second one, he has gained the wisdom from contemplation
but not tranquilization of the mind.
And the third one is one who has neither of these two things
and the fourth one is one who has both of these things.
And from here you can see the Buddha says
that if a person has tranquilization,
he should make an effort to establish the tranquilization
to make it stable, to make it strong,
and to attain the other that he has not attained
is to attain the wisdom from contemplation.
And the second case, one who has the wisdom from contemplation
but not tranquilization, he should make an effort
to establish his wisdom from contemplation
and to attain tranquilization also.
So in the third case, he should put forth an effort
to attain both tranquilization as well as contemplation,
the wisdom from contemplation.
One thing you note here is that the tranquilization
has to be established,
it is not perfect yet.
And same with contemplation,
the wisdom from contemplation here is not the perfect wisdom
that will make that person an Arahant.
So the Buddha says he has to make it,
to establish it or to attain it.
Samatha here, Samatha is tranquilization,
and vipassana or contemplation leads to panya.
Sometimes people nowadays tend to say that
this is such a thing as vipassana jana,
but vipassana jana is actually kanika samadhi,
and it's contemplation of one object after another.
So it is actually contemplation, it is not samadhi.
And it is not the Buddha samadhi because
we note that vipassana or what they call vipassana jana
is contemplation of one state after another,
and that is vipassana.
But samatha is the tranquilization of the mind
from contemplating one object,
one object and that leads to one pointedness of mind samadhi.
That's why we find, for example, in the Aryan Eightfold Path,
you have sati plus samadhi.
There are two different factors, they are not one factor.
The seventh factor is right mindfulness.
This is contemplation that is vipassana practice,
and that leads to right samadhi.
And it is very important to note that these two are two factors,
it's not one factor.
It's always separate that vipassana or the mindfulness
is one factor whereas samadhi is the ability
to put the mind on one thing only and let it stay on that one thing.
So that's why in a sutra like this,
it's quite clear that you have to develop both things,
the tranquilization as well as the contemplation.
And we can understand from the last part,
you see that they have to develop both these things
and after these two are attained
and he has to make a further effort to establish these two states
and use it to destroy the asavas.
There's one sutra later on we'll come across
for Angutra Nikhaya 4.170
where Venerable Ananda says there are four ways
to attain arahanhood, only four ways.
The first one is to practice samatha first followed by vipassana.
The second is vipassana first followed by samatha.
The third is samatha and vipassana at the same time.
The fourth is contemplation of the self
and that leads to one-pointedness of mind
and that leads to attainment of arahanhood
from further contemplation.
So you find that all these ways
have both the elements of samatha as well as vipassana.
